english: charge given them in words; in our translations introduced for apostle, pó-nya (pho nya) having been adopted for 'angel'. - mi-dpón (mi dpon) prefect Glr., C. - mi(i)-bu (mi(i) bu) 1. a child of man, a mortal, po., Mil., cf. mii bú-mo (mi'i bu mo) above. 2. son of man, when Christ speaks of himself as such, otherwise mii sras (mi'i sras) Chr. Prot. - mi-bo (mi bo) Cs., rarely for mi (mi). - mi-dbań (mi dbang), prince, potentate:-mí-ma-yín(-pa) (mi ma yin(-pa)) अमानुष, one that is not a human being, mi dań mi-ma-yín-pa t'ams-cád (mi dang mi ma yin pa thams cad) all human and not human (adversaries) Dom., esp. ghosts, demons, dur-k'ród-kyi mí-ma-yín-pa-rnams (dur khrod kyi mi ma yin pa rnams) the ghosts of a grave-yard (not the souls of the dead); mk'á-la rgyú-bai mi-ma-yín (mkha' la rgyu ba'i mi ma yin) the ghosts that walk in the air Mil.; dkár-p'yogs-kyi mí-ma yín-rnams (dkar phyogs kyi mi ma yin rnams) good genii Mil.; mi-ma-yín-gyi c'o-p'rúl (mi ma yin gyi cho 'phrul) apparitions of ghosts Mil. - mi-mo (mi mo) woman, yet only in contraposition to lhá-mo (lha mo) and other not human female beings Mil. and elsewh. - mi(i)-yúl (mi(i) yul) human world, lower world; earth, opp. to regions of the gods or of infernal beings Glr., Pth. - mi-rabs (mi rabs) mankind. - mi-rigs (mi rigs) v. rigs (rigs). - Mi-la-rás-pa (mi la ras pa), often only Mi-la (mi la), name of a Buddhist ascetic, of the 11 century (Wdk.), who between the periods of his meditations itinerating in the southern part of Middle Tibet as a mendicant friar, instructed the people by his improvisations delivered in poetry and song, brought the indifferent to his faith, refuted and converted the heretics, wrought manifold miracles (rdzu-p'rúl) (rdzu 'phrul), and whose legends, written not without wit and poetical merit, are still at the present day the most popular and widely circulated book in Tibet. - mi-lág (mi lag) servant, *mi-lág-tu ₒdó-wa* (mi lag tu 'do wa) to do servant's work, to perform drudgery W. - mi-lús (mi lus) 1. the human body. 2. v. lús-pa (lus pa). - mi-ser (mi ser) 1. subject, servant, menial, drudge. 2. robber, thief, sharper. - 3. v. below. III. negative adv.: not, in all such cases where ma (ma) (q.v.) is not used. With simple verbs the place of the negation is always immediately before them, in compound forms gen. before the last of the component parts, e.g. ₒbyúń-bar mi ₒgyúr-ro ('byung bar mi 'gyur ro), unless logically it belongs to the first, in which case often ma (ma) inst. of mi (mi) is employed. This rule, however, is not always strictly observed, so Glr. 70: de dań nám-du yań mi ₒbrál-bar gyis śig (de dang nam du yang mi 'bral bar gyis shig), and immediately after: skad ycíg kyań ma brál-bar gyis śig (skad gcig kyang ma bral bar gyis shig) do never part with it
and ཏང་གུད་ Tanggud, names of two provinces closely connected with each other, situated in the north-eastern part of Tibet and forming in ancient times a separate kingdom Glr.
1. n.p., formed after مصر mi-sér yu' (mi ser yul) Egypt, mi-sér-pa (mi ser pa) Egyptian, Chr. Prot. - 2. v. mi (mi).
Ssk. किन्नर, fabulous beings of Indian origin, nearly related to the ₒdri-za ('dri za), and belonging to the retinue of Kuvera; fem miam-ci-mo (mi'am ci mo).
1. a little man, dwarf, also miu-t'úń (mi'u thung) Wdn.; mig-gi miu (mig gi mi'u) v. mig (mig). - 2. perh. applicable also to puppet, doll.

resp. 1. eye. - 2. eye of a needle; hole in a hatchet or hammer, to insert the handle - 3. symb. num.: 2. - mig-gi gańs (mig gi gangs) Sch., the white of the eye; mig-gi rgyál-mo (mig gi rgyal mo) or miu (mi'u), 'the queen or the little man in the eye': 1. pupil. 2. iris Stg.; mig-gi snág-ts'a (mig gi snag tsha) or -mts'o (-mtsho) Cs., vulgo mig-gi nág-po (mig gi nag po) id.; mig-gi mé-tog (mig gi me tog) Sch. the luminous point of the eye: mig nyáms-pa (mig nyams pa) Cs. weak eyes; mig ltá-ba (mig lta ba) to see with the eyes, to look up, to look round Glr.; mig ₒdzúm-pa (mig 'dzum pa) to shut the eyes, ₒbyéd-pa ('byed pa) to open the eyes, v. ₒbyéd-pa ('byed pa) 1; ₒdón-pa ('don pa), ₒbyín-pa ('byin pa) to cut or tear out the eyes, to squeeze them out by a particular instrument, as a torture or punishment C.; mig bcár-ba (mig bcar ba) Lex., acc. to Sch. id.; mdóńs-pa (mdongs pa), mdóńs-par ₒgyúr-ba (mdongs par 'gyur ba) to get blind or blinded, to be deprived of sight Dzl.; mig ₒkyíd-pa (mig 'kyid pa) Sch., to distort or roll the eyes; mig skú-ba (mig sku ba) Dom. (bskú-ba?) (bsku ba?) n. of a certain magic trick; mig c'íd-pa (mig chid pa) inflammation of the eyes through cold, snow-blindness C. (perh. p'yíd-pa) (phyid pa); *mig zug soń* (mig zug song)
